item.title: No vote for now
item.content:encoded:
This is very charming. There are some issues, though.

How do you permanently close a building but also operate a storefront? This is probably the worst problem.

It's never said what -2 is. It's clear enough, but it is a slip of style nonetheless.

Addendum 2 suddenly switches to present tense, and Addendum 3 is also in present tense. Why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a little on the fence on this one but after considering the implications of 1254-2 I had to upvote. Is it to communicate or is it the survivors equivalent of a war mech/titan? The Foundation will only know when it's complete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FUCKING GENIUS. My headcanon right here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because authors have a terrible sense of scale. Wouldn't it make sense for their creations to be the same?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One guy nailed it. Indian in the Cupboard. This has so much potential.

What if other places had other instances? Instances that were bought by consumers who didn't know of its anomalous properties? Now that's thought.

Like a library. Dear god the implications with all those other books…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, and I think it is an idea that would be great to expand on. While the concept of little humanoids seems a bit overdone, I was impressed by the unique direction you took it.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if the bomb(s) was/were constructed from a collection of MAD Magazines, or perhaps some MAD Libs…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that book-magedeon kicked off in YA. I'm choosing to believe that the neighboring fantasy, horror, and romance civilizations formed a coalition and attempted to permanently remove the cultural contamination seeping into their borderlands from all those copies of Twilight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me when I realized the bomb was the cutest MAD ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. This is really neat. Plus all the ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Their average height was approximately 5 mm.

Would one hollowed out row of books in a standard bookshelf be large enough to host an entire civilization of 5 mm tall people?

Trace radiation readings, as well as the remains of various types of weaponry, suggest the fire was started as the result of a miniature thermonuclear war between one or more of the SCP-1254 civilizations, which proceeded to wipe out the entire SCP-1254-1 population.

Even scaled down that far, wouldn't a thermonuclear weapon have blown up the entire store, rather than just start a fire?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That they can turn paper into other materials saved this from "but the picture isn't anomalous" land. This is a lot of fun, and the creation of -2 lends a nice little "what could be it?" to the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation is probably scattered throughout the land of fiction. A bit in Fanfic, a lot in vanity publishing, some in horror, mystery, comedy… it's probably one big mess to keep together.

And yes, even a bit in Racy Novel, sad as that is. Speedy Muffler would be proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm years behind though, only picked it up a few months ago and I'm only halfway through One of Our Thursdays is Missing. Is the answer to the riddle to cross back from fanfiction ever explained?

Also good lord I wonder what the SCP section of the bookworld would be like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…you know about Thursday Next.

MARRY ME!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually it wasn't a snarky comment at all, it was a joke reference to the Thursday Next series, which includes a world inside books where people speak Lorem Ipsum as one of the languages (which is stock Latin as you may know.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As people have said before, somewhat iffy toe, but well done otherwise. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Broken” is not capitalized in said phrase, and while it may be a term that deviates from clinical tone, it successfully conveys the idea of something incomplete or that was interrupted.

I find your snarky comment devoid of meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for miniature nuclear warfare among other things

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's quite an interesting idea. If those people represent various sections of the written works of humanity, and they all seem to be quite mad, what does it say about us? The tone was a bit spotty at places, but nothing major. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The numbering scheme doesn't seem completely clear to me. You say that SCP-1254 refers to the stock of books in the bookshop, but the containment procedures make it seem like SCP-1254 is the bookshop itself, or elsewhere the civilisations inside the books. Also, I can't find anything that actually states what SCP-1254-2 is. That combined with a nondescript concept and some awkward wordings adds up to a reluctant downvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Broken Latin phrases? Do they speak Lorem Ipsum then? With some Courier Bold on the side?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure that I care for SCP-1254-2, but not enough to hurt my fondness for the rest. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like you Foundationized a kids tale like The Indian in the Cupboard. I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a cool and unique take on the "World in Miniature" trope, I dig. I'm also a sucker for supposedly neutralized SCPs bouncing back, and for a careful and academically-minded Foundation. Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept isn't very special; it's reminiscent of Adventure Time and an old gag-parade cartoon short I remember where the books on a shelf started chatting.1 And the main twist is the "world in miniature" territory that other SCPs have trod before.

But the execution does enough with it in terms of plot and imagery that I was amused and edged into an upvote.

Footnotes
1. The only feature that actually sticks in my memory is that one of the books was Peter Lorre. That sort of short always had Peter Lorre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone is wonky in places, but the idea is quite good and the execution is solid. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been waiting for someone to use that picture for a while now. Such a good one. Good article too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me a lot of SCP-1235, and that is a very good thing. I love miniature worlds.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ability or technology to convert paper into any conceivable material.

No, but a person with skin made out of some weird unbreakable sci-fi metal that can use magic, guns, and lord knows what else is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanna know what they're planning on doing with the paper man. I don't think they realize paper is, uh… not that… dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see one of my visual record pictures being put to use in a good article. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Post-apocalyptic paper people? I dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since they live in different genres… I guess you could call them a generic civilization?

Anyway. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, especially the idea of each civilization being associated with a genre. There are some minor tonal issues though.

Archeological analysis of the few remaining intact samples of SCP-1254 suggests that SCP-1254-1 possessed the ability or technology to convert paper into any conceivable material.

There is even evidence of the existence of organic foodstuffs.

There is definite proof that these civilizations were aware of and interacted with each other, with "border books" exhibiting a blend of various architectural styles that neighboring samples of SCP-1254 lacked.

witnessed moving through a destroyed copy of Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows, possibly scavenging for supplies.
